title: Practice Set 5 — Adding and Transforming Columns
description: Apply column transformation techniques: type casting with silent null detection, string cleaning, date extraction, arithmetic, conditional logic with when/otherwise, boolean flags, and a complete enrichment pipeline.

datasets.description: NordGrid raw Bronze meter readings with messy string data. 100 rows. All columns are strings — daily_kw and date need casting. Region values have mixed case and whitespace.

columns.name: daily_kw
columns.description: Daily energy as a raw string — includes numbers, empty strings, and unparseable values like 'N/A' and 'ERR'

columns.name: region
columns.description: Region code with inconsistent casing ('NORTH', 'north', 'North') and whitespace ('  NORTH', 'EAST  ')

characteristics.unparseableValues: 4 rows have daily_kw values that cannot be cast to double: 'N/A', 'ERR', '--', 'null'
characteristics.negatives: 3 rows have negative daily_kw values (as strings)
characteristics.totalNullsAfterCast: 8 rows will have null daily_kw after casting (4 empty + 4 unparseable)
characteristics.messyRegions: 14 distinct raw values including 'NORTH', 'north', 'North', '  NORTH', 'NORTH  ', etc.

tasks.title: Cast NordGrid's Raw Readings to Proper Types

description.context: NordGrid's Bronze meter CSV stores every column as a string. The Silver pipeline must cast daily_kw to DoubleType and date to DateType before any calculations or date operations can work. However, some daily_kw values are not valid numbers — they contain strings like 'N/A' and 'ERR' from the source system. Spark will silently convert these to null during the cast.
description.task: Read PS5_data_readings_raw.csv (header=True, no inferSchema — let all columns remain as strings). Then: (1) count how many nulls exist in daily_kw before casting and store in null_before; (2) cast daily_kw to double using withColumn and cast(); (3) cast date to date type using withColumn and cast(); (4) count how many nulls exist in daily_kw after casting and store in null_after. Store the casted DataFrame in a variable called df_typed.
description.validationHint: Before casting, there are 4 empty-string rows that read as null. After casting, 4 additional unparseable values ('N/A', 'ERR', '--', 'null') also become null — total 8.

starterScaffold.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col

spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName('nordgrid-type-casting') \
    .master('local[*]') \
    .getOrCreate()

# Read as raw strings — no inferSchema
df = spark.read.csv('PS5_data_readings_raw.csv', header=True)
print(f"All columns are strings: {[f.dataType.simpleString() for f in df.schema.fields]}")

# YOUR SOLUTION BELOW



# YOUR SOLUTION ABOVE

# Verification (do not modify)
print(f"Nulls before cast: {null_before}")
print(f"Nulls after cast:  {null_after}")
print(f"Silent nulls introduced: {null_after - null_before}")
df_typed.printSchema()
df_typed.show(5)

assertions.description: df_typed exists and is a DataFrame
daily_kw is now DoubleType
date is now DateType
null_before is 4 (empty strings read as null)
null_after is 8 (4 original + 4 silent nulls from unparseable values)

tasks.partialCredit: 5 independent assertions. Each passed assertion earns 1/5 credit.

tasks.title: Standardize NordGrid's Region Codes

description.context: NordGrid's source CSV exports region codes with inconsistent formatting. The same region appears as 'NORTH', 'north', 'North', '  NORTH', and 'NORTH  ' across different batch files. Downstream joins with the substation reference table fail silently because ' NORTH' does not match 'NORTH'. The Silver pipeline must standardize all region values to uppercase with no leading or trailing whitespace.
description.task: Read PS5_data_readings_raw.csv (header=True, inferSchema=True). Apply trim() and upper() to the region column using withColumn() to standardize all values. Store the result in a variable called df_clean. Then count the distinct region values and store the count in a variable called region_count.
description.validationHint: Before cleaning, there are 14 distinct region values. After cleaning, there should be exactly 4: NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST.

starterScaffold.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, upper, trim

spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName('nordgrid-region-cleanup') \
    .master('local[*]') \
    .getOrCreate()

df = spark.read.csv('PS5_data_readings_raw.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)
print(f"Distinct raw regions: {df.select('region').distinct().count()}")

# YOUR SOLUTION BELOW



# YOUR SOLUTION ABOVE

# Verification (do not modify)
print(f"Distinct clean regions: {region_count}")
df_clean.select('region').distinct().show()

assertions.description: df_clean is a DataFrame
Exactly 4 distinct region values after cleaning
All region values are uppercase
No region values have leading or trailing whitespace

tasks.partialCredit: 4 independent assertions. Each passed assertion earns 1/4 credit.

description.context: NordGrid classifies meter readings into consumption tiers using a when().otherwise() chain. A junior engineer writes the tier logic but is unsure how edge values and null readings are handled.
description.task: Read the tier assignment code below and predict which tier each sample reading receives. Pay attention to the order of conditions and what happens when daily_kw is null.
description.validationHint: The when() chain evaluates from top to bottom and returns the first matching condition. If no condition matches and otherwise() is present, the otherwise value is used. If daily_kw is null, all comparisons evaluate to null (not true), so no when() condition matches.

evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, when

df = df.withColumn('tier',
    when(col('daily_kw') < 500, 'low')
    .when(col('daily_kw') < 2000, 'medium')
    .when(col('daily_kw') < 4000, 'high')
    .otherwise('critical')
)

# Sample readings to classify:
# A: daily_kw = 499.9
# B: daily_kw = 500.0
# C: daily_kw = 2000.0
# D: daily_kw = 0.0
# E: daily_kw = null
# F: daily_kw = -50.0

fields.label: Reading B (daily_kw = 500.0): which tier?
fields.rationale: 500.0 < 500 → false. 500.0 < 2000 → true → returns 'medium'. The boundary value 500 falls into the next tier because the first condition uses strict less-than.
fields.distractorRationale: Students might pick 'low' thinking the boundary is inclusive.

fields.label: Reading E (daily_kw = null): which tier?
fields.rationale: null < 500 → null (not true). null < 2000 → null (not true). null < 4000 → null (not true). No condition matched → otherwise('critical'). The trap: null falls through all conditions to otherwise. If the engineer expected nulls to be excluded, they need to handle null explicitly before the when() chain or add a when(col('daily_kw').isNull(), 'unknown') as the first condition.
fields.distractorRationale: Students might pick 'null' expecting the tier itself to be null, but otherwise() catches everything that didn't match — including null rows.

fields.label: Reading F (daily_kw = -50.0): which tier?
fields.rationale: -50.0 < 500 → true → returns 'low'. Negative values satisfy the first condition. The engineer's code does not handle negative readings — they get classified as 'low' alongside legitimate low-usage meters.
fields.distractorRationale: Students might expect an error or 'critical' for negative values, but the when chain has no explicit check for negatives.

tasks.title: Enrich NordGrid Readings with Temporal Fields

description.context: NordGrid's Silver layer requires temporal fields derived from the reading date: year, month, and quarter for partitioning and reporting. The operations team also needs a daily_mw column (daily_kw divided by 1,000) for capacity reports that use megawatts instead of kilowatts. All values should be rounded to 3 decimal places.
description.task: Read PS5_data_readings_raw.csv (header=True, inferSchema=True). Cast the date column to DateType. Then add four new columns using withColumn(): (1) year extracted from date, (2) month extracted from date, (3) quarter extracted from date, and (4) daily_mw computed as daily_kw / 1000, rounded to 3 decimal places. Store the result in a variable called df_enriched.
description.validationHint: All dates are in March 2026, so year should be 2026, month should be 3, and quarter should be 1 for every row. daily_mw should be roughly 0.080 to 4.500 for valid readings.

starterScaffold.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, year, month, quarter, round as spark_round

spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName('nordgrid-temporal-enrichment') \
    .master('local[*]') \
    .getOrCreate()

df = spark.read.csv('PS5_data_readings_raw.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)

# YOUR SOLUTION BELOW



# YOUR SOLUTION ABOVE

# Verification (do not modify)
df_enriched.printSchema()
df_enriched.select('date', 'year', 'month', 'quarter', 'daily_kw', 'daily_mw').show(5)

assertions.description: df_enriched is a DataFrame
date column is DateType
year column exists and all values are 2026
month column exists and all values are 3
quarter column exists and all values are 1
daily_mw column exists and equals daily_kw / 1000 rounded to 3 places

tasks.partialCredit: 6 independent assertions. Each passed assertion earns 1/6 credit.

description.context: A NordGrid engineer is reviewing transformation code from a colleague and encounters several patterns. The engineer needs to determine which statements are correct about how PySpark handles constants, failed casts, and boolean flags.
description.task: For each code snippet or statement, determine the correct outcome. Each question tests a specific transformation behavior from Module 5.
description.validationHint: Pay attention to what lit() does, what happens when cast fails, and how boolean columns interact with null values.

fields.label: Code: df.withColumn('source', 'bronze_meters') — what happens?
options: A column named 'source' is added with the value 'bronze_meters' in every row
A TypeError is raised because the second argument must be a Column expression — use lit('bronze_meters') instead
The column 'source' is created with null values because strings are not valid Column types
The string 'bronze_meters' is interpreted as a column name and Spark looks for a column by that name
fields.correctAnswer: A TypeError is raised because the second argument must be a Column expression — use lit('bronze_meters') instead
fields.distractorRationale: Option 1 is what the engineer intended but not what happens. Option 3 invents a silent failure. Option 4 is actually what happens with col('bronze_meters') not a bare string in withColumn.

fields.label: A daily_kw column contains the string 'N/A'. After df.withColumn('daily_kw', col('daily_kw').cast('double')), what value does that row's daily_kw contain?
options: The string 'N/A' — cast fails silently and keeps the original value
0.0 — Spark treats non-numeric strings as zero
null — Spark produces null when a cast cannot parse the value
An error — Spark raises a NumberFormatException
fields.correctAnswer: null — Spark produces null when a cast cannot parse the value
fields.distractorRationale: Option 1 is wrong because cast replaces the column. Option 2 is a common assumption. Option 4 would be true in Java/Python but Spark intentionally avoids errors and produces null instead.

fields.label: An is_valid boolean column is defined as: col('daily_kw').isNotNull() & (col('daily_kw') > 0). For a row where daily_kw is null, what is the value of is_valid?
options: false — because isNotNull() returns false and false & anything is false
null — because daily_kw > 0 evaluates to null and false & null is null
true — because the NOT in isNotNull catches the null
An error — you cannot combine isNotNull with > on a null value
fields.correctAnswer: false — because isNotNull() returns false and false & anything is false
fields.distractorRationale: Option 2 is a subtle trap — in SQL three-valued logic, false AND null is false (not null), because the result cannot be true regardless of the null. This is actually correct behavior that benefits the engineer. Option 3 misreads the logic. Option 4 is incorrect — PySpark handles this cleanly.

fields.label: withColumn('daily_kw', spark_round(col('daily_kw'), 2)) is called on a DataFrame where daily_kw already exists. What happens?
options: A second daily_kw column is added alongside the original
An error — you cannot use withColumn with an existing column name
The existing daily_kw column is replaced with the rounded values in a new DataFrame
The original DataFrame is modified in place with rounded values
fields.correctAnswer: The existing daily_kw column is replaced with the rounded values in a new DataFrame
fields.distractorRationale: Option 1 would create ambiguity. Option 2 is wrong — withColumn is designed for both add and replace. Option 4 contradicts immutability.

tasks.title: Build NordGrid's Complete Meter Enrichment Pipeline

description.context: NordGrid's data engineering team needs a complete enrichment pipeline that transforms raw Bronze meter readings into a fully typed, cleaned, and enriched Silver dataset. The pipeline must handle every data quality issue in the raw CSV: string columns that need casting, inconsistent region formatting, unparseable values that become silent nulls, and the addition of derived fields for downstream consumers.
description.task: Write a pipeline that: (1) reads PS5_data_readings_raw.csv as raw strings (header=True, no inferSchema); (2) casts daily_kw to double; (3) casts date to date type; (4) standardizes region with trim() and upper(); (5) adds a year column from the date; (6) adds a consumption_tier column using when/otherwise: 'low' for < 500, 'medium' for < 2000, 'high' for < 4000, 'critical' otherwise; (7) adds an is_valid boolean flag: true when daily_kw is not null AND daily_kw > 0 AND daily_kw < 10000; (8) adds a pipeline_version column with the constant integer 2 using lit(); (9) selects the final output: meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, year, consumption_tier, is_valid, pipeline_version. Store the result in a variable called silver.
description.validationHint: The output should have 100 rows and 8 columns. The is_valid flag should be false for null, negative, zero, and extreme (>= 10000) readings. Null readings should get consumption_tier = 'critical' (they fall through all when conditions to otherwise).

starterScaffold.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, upper, trim, year, when, lit

spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName('nordgrid-enrichment-pipeline') \
    .master('local[*]') \
    .config('spark.sql.shuffle.partitions', '8') \
    .getOrCreate()

readings_path = 'PS5_data_readings_raw.csv'

# YOUR SOLUTION BELOW



# YOUR SOLUTION ABOVE

# Verification (do not modify)
silver.printSchema()
print(f"Row count: {silver.count()}")
print(f"Columns: {silver.columns}")
print(f"Valid readings: {silver.filter(col('is_valid')).count()}")
print(f"Invalid readings: {silver.filter(~col('is_valid')).count()}")
silver.show(5)

assertions.description: silver is a DataFrame
Exactly 8 columns in the correct order
daily_kw is DoubleType
date is DateType
All region values are standardized to exactly 4 distinct values
consumption_tier has no null values (otherwise catches everything)
is_valid is false for null daily_kw rows
pipeline_version is 2 for all rows (constant via lit())
Row count is 100 (no rows filtered — enrichment only)

tasks.partialCredit: 9 independent assertions. Each passed assertion earns 1/9 credit.

conceptsCombined: Type casting with cast() and silent null detection (Lesson 3)
String cleaning with upper() and trim() (Lesson 4)
Boolean flag columns for data quality (Lesson 8)
Final select for output schema contract (Lesson 10)
synthesisNotes.rationale: This task mirrors NordGrid's complete Bronze-to-Silver enrichment pipeline from Lesson 10. The student must combine every major technique from the module into a single pipeline. Key edge cases: (1) null daily_kw values fall through when() to 'critical' tier, (2) is_valid correctly handles nulls because isNotNull is checked first, (3) lit() is required for the constant pipeline_version column. No rows are filtered — this is a pure enrichment pipeline.

selfCheck.foundationModuleException: Not applicable — Module 5 is Core Mechanics phase.
selfCheck.theoryPracticeBoundary: Theory examples use pre-typed Silver data. The practice dataset is raw Bronze strings requiring casting — students must apply cast() themselves rather than starting from pre-typed data. The when/otherwise tier thresholds match the theory (500/2000/4000), but Task 3 tests edge values and null behavior that the theory describes but does not make students calculate. The region cleaning task uses messier data (14 variants vs 4 in the theory) to test transfer of upper()+trim().
selfCheck.datasetNotes: PS5_data_readings_raw.csv has 100 rows with intentionally messy data: all columns are strings, daily_kw has 4 unparseable values that become silent nulls on cast, and region has 14 raw variants that standardize to 4. This forces students to apply every cleaning technique from the module.
